description: Nurturing Engine Phase 1 — Shared package foundations: create @ortho/types package with lead.outbound_sent, lead.activity_logged event types and unenroll_sequence action type; create @ortho/interpolator package with interpolateFields (dot-notation + template string resolver) and computeNextActiveWindowMs (active-hours calculator, DST-safe) with full unit test coverage.

userStories.title: Create @ortho/interpolator package + interpolateFields implementation + unit tests
userStories.description: As the Nurturing Engine step worker, I need an interpolateFields function so that step action params with dot-notation paths and {{template}} tokens are resolved against the enrollment context before dispatch.
acceptanceCriteria: Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/package.json: name '@ortho/interpolator', version '0.1.0', type 'module', main 'dist/index.js', types 'dist/index.d.ts', scripts { build: 'tsc', typecheck: 'tsc --noEmit', test: 'vitest run', 'test:watch': 'vitest' }, devDependencies { typescript: '^5.0.0', '@types/node': '^22.0.0', vitest: '^2.0.0' }

Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/tsconfig.json: same compilerOptions as @ortho/event-bus (ES2022, NodeNext, strict, outDir dist, rootDir src, declaration true, declarationMap true, sourceMap true, esModuleInterop true, skipLibCheck true), include ['src/**/*'], exclude ['node_modules', 'dist']
Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/vitest.config.ts: import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config'; export default defineConfig({ test: { include: ['test/**/*.test.ts'] } })
Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/src/interpolate.ts with: const DOT_PATH_RE = /^[a-zA-Z_][\w.]*$/ and const TEMPLATE_TOKEN_RE = /\{\{(\w+)\}\}/g

src/interpolate.ts exports function getByPath(obj: Record<string, unknown>, path: string): unknown — splits path on '.', reduces to nested value; returns undefined for missing keys or non-object intermediaries

src/interpolate.ts exports function interpolateValue(value: unknown, context: Record<string, unknown>): unknown — if value is a string matching DOT_PATH_RE: return getByPath(context, value); if value is a string containing at least one {{token}}: replace each {{key}} with String(context[key]) if key present, or keep '{{key}}' literal if missing; if value is a non-string or matches neither pattern: return value unchanged

src/interpolate.ts exports function interpolateFields(params: Record<string, unknown>, context: Record<string, unknown>): Record<string, unknown> — recursively resolves all values: strings via interpolateValue; arrays via map; nested objects via recursive call; non-strings/non-objects returned as-is
Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/src/index.ts re-exporting interpolateValue and interpolateFields from './interpolate.js'
Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/test/interpolate.test.ts with the following test cases:
interpolateValue: dot-notation 'context.phone' against { context: { phone: '+1555' } } → '+1555'
interpolateValue: nested dot-notation 'a.b.c' against { a: { b: { c: 42 } } } → 42
interpolateValue: deeply nested missing path 'a.b.x' against { a: { b: { c: 1 } } } → undefined
interpolateValue: template string '{{enrollment_id}}-step-1' against { enrollment_id: 'abc123' } → 'abc123-step-1'
interpolateValue: template string with multiple tokens '{{entity_type}}/{{entity_id}}' against { entity_type: 'lead', entity_id: '456' } → 'lead/456'
interpolateValue: template string with missing key '{{missing}}-suffix' → '{{missing}}-suffix' (literal preserved)
interpolateValue: plain string 'literal_value' (no dot-notation, no template tokens) → 'literal_value' unchanged
interpolateValue: non-string number 99 → 99 unchanged
interpolateValue: non-string boolean false → false unchanged
interpolateValue: non-string null → null unchanged
interpolateFields: flat object { to: 'context.phone', dedup_key: '{{enrollment_id}}-step-1' } against { context: { phone: '+1999' }, enrollment_id: 'e1' } → { to: '+1999', dedup_key: 'e1-step-1' }
interpolateFields: nested object with array — { payload: { ids: ['context.id', 'literal'] } } against { context: { id: 'x1' } } → { payload: { ids: ['x1', 'literal'] } }
interpolateFields: non-string values in object are passed through unchanged
Run npm install from packages/@ortho/interpolator
Typecheck passes; Tests pass
userStories.notes: [Phase 1, originally US-002] The context object passed to interpolateFields is a merged root: { context: <enrollmentContext>, enrollment_id, step_id, entity_type, entity_id }. Dot-notation 'context.phone' resolves the 'context' key first, then 'phone' within it — this is why the enrollment context is stored under a 'context' key in the root. Template tokens like '{{enrollment_id}}' resolve top-level keys in the same root. This design means a single flat context object handles both resolution modes. TEMPLATE_TOKEN_RE is a global regex — reset lastIndex before each use or use a fresh regex per call to avoid stale lastIndex bugs. A string can contain both a template token AND look like a dot-notation path only if it has braces — DOT_PATH_RE will not match strings containing '{' or '}', so the two modes are mutually exclusive for any given value.

userStories.title: Add computeNextActiveWindowMs to @ortho/interpolator + unit tests
userStories.description: As the Nurturing Engine step worker, I need a computeNextActiveWindowMs function so that send_message and send_email steps can be deferred until the next active-hours window, with correct DST handling.
acceptanceCriteria: Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/src/active-hours.ts exporting: interface ActiveHoursConfig { start: string; end: string; } where both fields are HH:MM 24-hour strings
src/active-hours.ts exports: function computeNextActiveWindowMs(config: ActiveHoursConfig, timezone: string, now?: Date): number — now defaults to new Date()
Implementation: parse start/end to milliseconds-since-midnight via parseHHMM(time: string): number = (h*3600 + m*60) * 1000
Implementation: compute current time-of-day ms in target timezone using Intl.DateTimeFormat with timeZone option, hour/minute/second parts, hourCycle 'h23'; add date.getMilliseconds()
Implementation: validate timezone — try Intl.DateTimeFormat(undefined, { timeZone: tz }), catch → console.warn and fall back to 'UTC'
Implementation: if start === end → return 0 (always-open window)

Implementation: non-midnight-crossing window (startMs <= endMs): if currentMs >= startMs && currentMs < endMs → 0; if currentMs < startMs → startMs - currentMs; if currentMs >= endMs → DAY_MS - currentMs + startMs
Implementation: midnight-crossing window (startMs > endMs): if currentMs >= startMs || currentMs < endMs → 0; else → startMs - currentMs
Implementation: result is always in range [0, DAY_MS) — DAY_MS = 86_400_000
Export ActiveHoursConfig and computeNextActiveWindowMs from packages/@ortho/interpolator/src/index.ts (add to existing re-exports)
Create packages/@ortho/interpolator/test/active-hours.test.ts with the following test cases:
time currently inside window (08:00–20:00, now=10:00 in America/Chicago) → 0
time before window start (08:00–20:00, now=07:00 in America/Chicago) → exactly 3600000 ms (1 hour)
time after window end (08:00–20:00, now=21:00 in America/Chicago) → ms until 08:00 next day (11 hours = 39600000)
start === end → always returns 0 regardless of current time
invalid timezone string → does not throw, falls back to UTC, returns a non-negative number
midnight-crossing window (22:00–06:00): time=23:00 in UTC → 0 (inside window)
midnight-crossing window (22:00–06:00): time=10:00 in UTC → ms until 22:00 (12 hours = 43200000)
DST edge: America/New_York, window 08:00–20:00, now set to a UTC timestamp corresponding to 07:30 EST (before spring-forward on 2026-03-08) → result > 0 and result <= DAY_MS
result is never negative and never exceeds DAY_MS (86400000) — assert for multiple time positions
Typecheck passes; Tests pass
userStories.notes: [Phase 1, originally US-003] The active-hours calculator in the Automation Engine (apps/platform/automation/src/services/active-hours.ts) uses an identical algorithm — reference it for the implementation pattern. Key difference: computeNextActiveWindowMs takes a pre-resolved timezone string directly (the Nurturing Engine resolves timezone_field from context before calling this function), while the Automation Engine's computeDelay resolves the timezone internally. For DST tests, use fixed UTC timestamps that correspond to known local times: 2026-03-08T12:30:00Z = 07:30 EST before spring-forward. Use vitest fake timers (vi.setSystemTime) to control 'now' in tests that need specific times. The test for 'time after window end at 21:00' should use a specific UTC timestamp that maps to 21:00 in America/Chicago to be deterministic — compute it as e.g. new Date('2026-01-15T03:00:00Z') which is 21:00 CST (UTC-6 in January).

AND status = 'pending' RETURNING id; returns the returned id string or null if no row was updated (use db.raw or .where().update().returning pattern)

Method findOrphanedOverdueSteps(): Promise<SequenceStepExecution[]> — returns rows WHERE status = 'pending' AND scheduled_at < now() - interval '1 minute' AND job_id IS NOT NULL (for safety-net poller); use db.raw("now() - interval '1 minute'") for the timestamp comparison
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 2, originally US-004] insertMany with an empty array must not crash — add a guard: if rows.length === 0 return []. claimPending uses the optimistic lock pattern: the UPDATE touches a row only if status is still 'pending'; returns null when another worker already claimed it (no row returned). Methods that accept a trx parameter should use (trx ?? this.db) as the query builder so they work both inside and outside transactions. updateScheduledAt sets status back to 'pending' as well as clearing/updating job_id — this is intentional: when a step is deferred mid-execution the worker resets it to pending with new schedule. JSON.stringify is NOT needed for output in updateStatus — Knex/pg handles jsonb serialization for plain objects.

userStories.title: Add JWT auth plugin to nurturing service
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a Fastify JWT auth plugin with a requireRole prehandler factory so that management routes can enforce role-based access control using Identity Service JWTs.
acceptanceCriteria: Add '@fastify/jwt': '^9.0.0' to dependencies in apps/platform/nurturing/package.json and run npm install from that directory to update package-lock.json
Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/plugins/auth.ts
Plugin is a Fastify plugin exported as default async function authPlugin(fastify, opts) using FastifyPluginAsync type
Plugin reads JWT_SECRET from process.env['JWT_SECRET']; throws new Error('JWT_SECRET is required') if missing
Plugin registers @fastify/jwt with { secret: JWT_SECRET }
Plugin adds a fastify.authenticate prehandler using fastify.addHook or fastify.decorate: async (request, reply) => { try { await request.jwtVerify(); } catch (err) { reply.send(err); } }

Plugin exports (via fastify.decorate) a requireRole helper: requireRole(roles: string[]): prehandlerAsyncHookHandler — returns an async prehandler that reads request.user as { sub: string; role: string } (type assertion), throws fastify.httpErrors.forbidden('Insufficient role') if request.user.role is not in the roles array

Declare module augmentation in the same file: declare module 'fastify' { interface FastifyInstance { requireRole(roles: string[]): prehandlerAsyncHookHandler; } } — import prehandlerAsyncHookHandler from 'fastify'
Register authPlugin in apps/platform/nurturing/src/index.ts before route registration: await fastify.register(authPlugin)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-001] JWT payload shape is { sub: string; role: string } — the role field carries 'marketing_staff' | 'marketing_manager' | 'service'. The authenticate prehandler is used on all management routes; requireRole is applied additionally on activate/disable. For the module augmentation, prehandlerAsyncHookHandler is imported from 'fastify'. Do NOT use @fastify/sensible's httpErrors unless it's already registered — check src/index.ts; @fastify/sensible is registered there from Phase 2 so fastify.httpErrors.forbidden() is available. The plugin file must use .ts extension and NodeNext moduleResolution — use import type where applicable to avoid runtime issues.

userStories.title: GET /sequences list and GET /sequences/:id detail routes
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the sequence read routes so that clients can list sequences with pagination and retrieve a single sequence with its active and current version data.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/sequences.ts exporting a default Fastify plugin function: async function sequencesRoutes(fastify, opts)
The plugin accepts an opts object with typed properties: definitionsRepo: SequenceDefinitionsRepository, versionsRepo: SequenceVersionsRepository — define a SequencesRouteOptions interface for this

Register the plugin in apps/platform/nurturing/src/index.ts: import and register sequencesRoutes after auth plugin, passing repo instances constructed with the db instance; db is created via createDb() from src/db.ts

GET /sequences route: preHandler: [fastify.authenticate]; query schema (TypeBox): { limit: Type.Optional(Type.Integer({ minimum: 1, maximum: 100, default: 20 })), cursor: Type.Optional(Type.String()) }

GET /sequences handler: calls definitionsRepo.findAll({ limit, cursor }); for each definition, loads the version row via versionsRepo.findBySequenceAndVersion(def.id, def.current_version) to compute step_count (Array.isArray(version.steps) ? version.steps.length : 0, defaulting to 0 if version not found); returns 200 with array of { id, name, status, active_version, current_version, step_count, created_at }
GET /sequences/:id route: preHandler: [fastify.authenticate]; params schema (TypeBox): { id: Type.String({ format: 'uuid' }) }

GET /sequences/:id handler: calls definitionsRepo.findById(id); if null, reply.code(404).send({ error: 'sequence_not_found' }); loads active version row via versionsRepo.findBySequenceAndVersion(id, def.active_version) if def.active_version != null (else null); loads current version row via versionsRepo.findBySequenceAndVersion(id, def.current_version); returns 200 with { ...def, active_version_data: activeVersion, current_version_data: currentVersion }
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-002] The routes file takes repos as plugin opts — this is the same dependency-injection pattern used in the automation engine routes. The db instance is created once in src/index.ts and passed to all repos and route plugins. Import SequenceDefinitionsRepository and SequenceVersionsRepository from their repo files. Use @sinclair/typebox Type helpers for schema validation — import { Type, Static } from '@sinclair/typebox'. For the route schema, set schema: { querystring: QuerySchema } etc. The step_count is derived by parsing the 'steps' jsonb field — the repo returns it as unknown so cast via (version.steps as unknown[]) after checking Array.isArray. Fastify 5 uses the 'preHandler' key (camelCase) in route options.

userStories.title: POST /sequences create route
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the sequence creation route so that clients can create a new sequence definition with an initial draft version 1.
acceptanceCriteria: Add POST /sequences route to apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/sequences.ts
preHandler: [fastify.authenticate]
Body schema (TypeBox): { name: Type.String({ minLength: 1, maxLength: 255 }) }
Handler reads created_by from request.user.sub (string)
Handler calls definitionsRepo.create({ name, created_by }) to insert the sequence definition row (status: 'draft', current_version: 1, active_version: null)
Handler calls versionsRepo.insert({ sequence_id: def.id, version: 1, steps: [], cancel_on_opt_out: true, created_by }) to insert version 1 with empty steps array
Returns 201 with { id: def.id, name: def.name, status: def.status, current_version: def.current_version, active_version: def.active_version, created_at: def.created_at }
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-003] No transaction needed here — if version insert fails after definition insert, the orphaned definition row is harmless (will appear as a draft with no version). The versionsRepo.insert already handles JSON.stringify for steps. The created_by comes from the JWT sub claim — request.user is typed as { sub: string; role: string } per the module augmentation from US-001.

userStories.title: Versioning service and PUT /sequences/:id save-draft route
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the versioning service and PUT route so that sequence drafts can be saved by inserting a new version row and bumping current_version, while leaving active_version unchanged.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/versioning.service.ts
Export interface SaveDraftInput: { active_hours?: unknown; cancel_on_opt_out?: boolean; steps: unknown[]; ab_test?: unknown }
Export class VersioningService with constructor(private readonly definitionsRepo: SequenceDefinitionsRepository, private readonly versionsRepo: SequenceVersionsRepository)
Method saveDraft(sequenceId: string, input: SaveDraftInput, createdBy?: string): Promise<{ definition: SequenceDefinition; version: SequenceVersion }>
saveDraft: loads definition via definitionsRepo.findById(sequenceId); throws error with message 'sequence_not_found' if null
saveDraft: computes newVersion = definition.current_version + 1

saveDraft: calls versionsRepo.insert({ sequence_id: sequenceId, version: newVersion, active_hours: input.active_hours, cancel_on_opt_out: input.cancel_on_opt_out ?? true, steps: input.steps, ab_test: input.ab_test, created_by: createdBy })
saveDraft: calls definitionsRepo.updateCurrentVersion(sequenceId, newVersion) to bump current_version
saveDraft: returns { definition: updatedDefinition, version: newVersionRow } where updatedDefinition is the result of updateCurrentVersion
Add PUT /sequences/:id route in apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/sequences.ts
preHandler: [fastify.authenticate]
Params schema (TypeBox): { id: Type.String({ format: 'uuid' }) }
Body schema (TypeBox): { active_hours: Type.Optional(Type.Unknown()), cancel_on_opt_out: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean()), steps: Type.Array(Type.Unknown()), ab_test: Type.Optional(Type.Unknown()) }
Update SequencesRouteOptions interface to add versioningService: VersioningService
Update route plugin registration in src/index.ts to pass versioningService: new VersioningService(definitionsRepo, versionsRepo)
PUT handler: calls versioningService.saveDraft(id, body, request.user.sub); on 'sequence_not_found' error, reply 404; returns 200 with { definition, version }
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-004] active_version is NOT changed by saveDraft — it stays at whatever it was set to previously (null or a prior version number). Only POST /sequences/:id/activate changes active_version. The VersioningService is a plain class — no Fastify coupling. Import SequenceDefinition and SequenceVersion types from their respective repo files. For the 'sequence_not_found' error pattern, the handler catches the thrown error and checks error.message === 'sequence_not_found' to reply 404. Type.Unknown() in TypeBox accepts any JSON value — appropriate for DSL fields (active_hours, ab_test) that are validated at runtime by the step worker, not at the API layer.

userStories.title: POST /sequences/:id/activate and POST /sequences/:id/disable lifecycle routes
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the activate and disable routes so that marketing managers can promote a sequence draft to active and pause a sequence, with role enforcement.
acceptanceCriteria: Add POST /sequences/:id/activate route in apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/sequences.ts
preHandler: [fastify.authenticate, fastify.requireRole(['marketing_manager'])]
Params schema (TypeBox): { id: Type.String({ format: 'uuid' }) }
Handler: calls definitionsRepo.findById(id); if null, reply 404 with { error: 'sequence_not_found' }
Handler: calls definitionsRepo.setActiveVersion(id, def.current_version) — sets active_version = current_version AND status = 'active'
Handler: returns 200 with updated SequenceDefinition row
Add POST /sequences/:id/disable route in apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/sequences.ts
preHandler: [fastify.authenticate, fastify.requireRole(['marketing_manager'])]
Params schema (TypeBox): { id: Type.String({ format: 'uuid' }) }
Handler: calls definitionsRepo.findById(id); if null, reply 404 with { error: 'sequence_not_found' }
Handler: calls definitionsRepo.updateStatus(id, 'disabled')
Handler: returns 200 with updated SequenceDefinition row
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-005] requireRole(['marketing_manager']) is the prehandler decorator added in US-001 — it reads request.user.role and throws 403 if the role is not in the allowed list. The preHandler array runs authenticate first (sets request.user) then requireRole (checks it). setActiveVersion sets both active_version and status = 'active' in a single UPDATE — this was defined in the SequenceDefinitionsRepository.setActiveVersion method from Phase 2 US-003. After disable, in-progress enrollments continue normally — the disable only blocks new enrollments (enforced by the enrollment manager in Phase 4).

userStories.title: Integration tests: CRUD lifecycle, versioning, and role enforcement
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need integration tests covering the full sequence management API lifecycle so that correctness of routes, versioning logic, and role enforcement is verifiable.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/test/integration/sequences-api.test.ts

Test setup: build a Fastify app instance using the same factory as src/index.ts (extract app building into a helper or call the same index bootstrap with TEST_PORT); set process.env['JWT_SECRET'] = 'test-secret' and process.env['DATABASE_URL'] before tests; use beforeAll/afterAll to start/stop the server
Helper: mintJwt(role: string): string — uses jsonwebtoken or fastify.jwt.sign({ sub: 'test-user', role }) to produce a signed token for test requests
Test: POST /sequences with valid JWT → 201, response has id, name, status='draft', current_version=1, active_version=null
Test: GET /sequences with valid JWT → 200, response array includes the newly created sequence with step_count=0
Test: GET /sequences/:id with valid JWT → 200, response has current_version_data with steps=[] and active_version_data=null

Test: PUT /sequences/:id with valid JWT and body { steps: [{ id: 'step-1', delay: { value: 24, unit: 'hours' }, action: { type: 'send_message', params: {} } }] } → 200, response.definition.current_version=2, response.definition.active_version remains null, response.version.version=2
Test: GET /sequences/:id after PUT → current_version_data.version=2 with 1 step, active_version_data=null
Test: POST /sequences/:id/activate with marketing_manager JWT → 200, response.active_version=2, response.status='active'
Test: POST /sequences/:id/activate with marketing_staff JWT → 403
Test: POST /sequences/:id/activate with no Authorization header → 401
Test: POST /sequences/:id/disable with marketing_manager JWT → 200, response.status='disabled'
Test: POST /sequences/:id/disable with marketing_staff JWT → 403
Test: GET /sequences/:id for non-existent UUID → 404 with { error: 'sequence_not_found' }
Test: PUT /sequences/:id for non-existent UUID → 404 with { error: 'sequence_not_found' }

All tests must use a DATABASE_URL pointing to a real PostgreSQL instance with the platform_nurturing schema migrated; skip all tests with test.skipIf(!process.env['DATABASE_URL'])('...') if DATABASE_URL is not set
Each test run uses a unique sequence name (e.g. append Date.now()) to avoid cross-test collisions without requiring full DB teardown
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 3, originally US-006] To avoid having to refactor src/index.ts, create apps/platform/nurturing/test/helpers/build-app.ts that imports and calls the same Fastify setup (register plugins + routes) returning the fastify instance without calling fastify.listen() — tests inject directly via fastify.inject(). To mint JWTs in tests without running a server first, use the 'jsonwebtoken' package (add as devDependency if not present) with the same JWT_SECRET value. The test.skipIf pattern in Vitest: import { it, describe, beforeAll } from 'vitest'; use describe.skipIf(!process.env['DATABASE_URL'])('sequences API integration', () => { ... }). Use a beforeAll to run migrations before the test suite if DATABASE_URL is set — run knex.migrate.latest() using the knexfile config. Each test should clean up its own created sequence using a direct DB call in afterEach or afterAll to keep the DB tidy between runs.

userStories.title: Install BullMQ + ioredis and create step queue module
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need BullMQ installed and a step queue module — and a createApp factory — so that the enrollment manager can enqueue delayed step jobs and tests can inject a mock queue without a real Redis.
acceptanceCriteria: Add 'bullmq': '^5.0.0' and 'ioredis': '^5.0.0' to dependencies in apps/platform/nurturing/package.json and run npm install from that directory
Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/queue/step-queue.ts
Export type StepJobData: { enrollment_id: string; step_execution_id: string; step_id: string; step_index: number }

Export function createStepQueue(redisUrl: string): Queue<StepJobData> — creates a BullMQ Queue named 'nurturing:step-execution' using new IORedis(redisUrl, { maxRetriesPerRequest: null, enableReadyCheck: false }) as the connection

Refactor apps/platform/nurturing/src/index.ts: extract app setup into export async function createApp(opts?: { queue?: Queue<StepJobData> | null }): Promise<FastifyInstance> — registers all plugins and routes, returns the Fastify instance without calling fastify.listen()

Keep a default export or named export async function main() that calls createApp(), reads REDIS_URL from process.env (logs a warning if missing and passes null as queue), calls fastify.listen(), and handles process signals
Inside createApp: if opts.queue is provided use it; otherwise if REDIS_URL env var is set create via createStepQueue(REDIS_URL); otherwise use null
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 4, originally US-001] BullMQ v5 requires IORedis with { maxRetriesPerRequest: null } — omitting this causes worker connection errors. The createApp factory is the same pattern used in the automation engine: tests call createApp({ queue: mockQueue }) and inject requests via fastify.inject() without starting the HTTP server. Import Queue from 'bullmq'; import IORedis from 'ioredis'. The queue instance will be threaded into the EnrollmentManager in US-005 when routes are registered.

userStories.title: Extend enrollment and step-execution repositories with methods needed by enrollment manager
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the enrollment and step-execution repos to expose typed methods for insert, dedup lookup, pagination, and job-id writeback so that the enrollment manager can persist data transactionally.
acceptanceCriteria: In apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/enrollments.repo.ts, export type SequenceEnrollment with fields: id (string), sequence_id (string), sequence_version (number), entity_type (string), entity_id (string), context (Record<string, unknown>), ab_variant (string | null), status (string), enrolled_at (Date), completed_at (Date | null), dedup_key (string)

Export type InsertEnrollmentData: { sequence_id: string; sequence_version: number; entity_type: string; entity_id: string; context: Record<string, unknown>; ab_variant: string | null; status: string; enrolled_at: Date; dedup_key: string }
Add method findByDedupKey(dedupKey: string): Promise<SequenceEnrollment | null> — SELECT * FROM sequence_enrollments WHERE dedup_key = ? LIMIT 1

Add method insert(data: InsertEnrollmentData, trx?: Knex.Transaction): Promise<SequenceEnrollment> — INSERT INTO sequence_enrollments VALUES (...) RETURNING *; uses (trx ?? this.db) as the query builder

Add method findBySequenceId(sequenceId: string, opts: { limit: number; cursor?: string }): Promise<SequenceEnrollment[]> — SELECT WHERE sequence_id = ? [AND enrolled_at < cursor] ORDER BY enrolled_at DESC LIMIT limit; cursor is an ISO timestamp string decoded as new Date(cursor)
Add method findById(id: string): Promise<SequenceEnrollment | null> — SELECT WHERE id = ? LIMIT 1

In apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/step-executions.repo.ts, export type SequenceStepExecution with fields: id (string), enrollment_id (string), step_id (string), step_index (number), scheduled_at (Date), job_id (string | null), status (string), attempt (number), output (unknown | null), error (string | null), started_at (Date | null), completed_at (Date | null)
Export type InsertStepExecutionData: { enrollment_id: string; step_id: string; step_index: number; scheduled_at: Date; job_id: null; status: 'pending'; attempt: 0 }

Add method insertMany(data: InsertStepExecutionData[], trx?: Knex.Transaction): Promise<SequenceStepExecution[]> — INSERT INTO sequence_step_executions (...) VALUES ... RETURNING *; uses (trx ?? this.db); handle empty array by returning [] without querying
Add method updateJobId(id: string, jobId: string): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET job_id = ? WHERE id = ?
Add method findByEnrollmentId(enrollmentId: string): Promise<SequenceStepExecution[]> — SELECT WHERE enrollment_id = ? ORDER BY step_index ASC
Add method findByEnrollmentAndStepId(enrollmentId: string, stepId: string): Promise<SequenceStepExecution | null> — SELECT WHERE enrollment_id = ? AND step_id = ? LIMIT 1
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 4, originally US-002] Import 'import type { Knex } from "knex"' for the trx parameter type. The pattern (trx ?? this.db) means all methods work both inside and outside a Knex transaction. For insertMany, Knex 3 batch insert: db('table').insert(arrayOfRows).returning('*') returns the inserted rows typed as any[] — cast to SequenceStepExecution[]. The context field is JSONB in Postgres; Knex returns it already parsed as an object, so no JSON.parse needed. The existing repo classes from Phase 2 likely already have a constructor taking a Knex instance (this.db) — add these methods to those existing classes without restructuring.

userStories.title: A/B variant assigner service with unit tests
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need an A/B variant assigner so that enrollments can be probabilistically assigned to a test variant using weighted random selection.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/ab-assigner.ts
Export type AbTestConfig: { enabled: boolean; split: Record<string, number> }
Export function assignVariant(abTest: AbTestConfig | null | undefined): string | null
If abTest is null, undefined, or abTest.enabled === false, return null
If all split weights are 0 or split has no entries, return null

Implementation: compute total = sum of all weight values in split; generate r = Math.random() * total; iterate Object.entries(split) accumulating weight; return the first key where accumulated weight > r
Create apps/platform/nurturing/test/unit/ab-assigner.test.ts
Test: assignVariant(null) returns null
Test: assignVariant(undefined) returns null
Test: assignVariant({ enabled: false, split: { A: 50, B: 50 } }) returns null
Test: assignVariant({ enabled: true, split: { A: 100, B: 0 } }) called 100 times — all results equal 'A'
Test: assignVariant({ enabled: true, split: { A: 0, B: 100 } }) called 100 times — all results equal 'B'
Test: assignVariant({ enabled: true, split: { A: 50, B: 50 } }) called 10000 times — both 'A' and 'B' appear; count for each is between 4000 and 6000
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 4, originally US-003] The accumulation loop: let acc = 0; for (const [key, weight] of Object.entries(split)) { acc += weight; if (r < acc) return key; } — the strict '<' comparison handles 0-weight entries naturally (acc never advances past their range). The ab_test column is stored as jsonb and returned from Knex as unknown — callers must cast to AbTestConfig | null before passing to assignVariant. No randomness seeding is needed in tests — with 10000 samples the 40-60% bounds are extremely robust.

userStories.title: Enrollment Manager service with unit tests
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the enrollment manager so that entities can be enrolled in sequences with dedup checking, status validation, transactional DB writes, and post-commit BullMQ job scheduling.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/enrollment-manager.ts
Export type EnrollInput: { sequence_id: string; entity_type: string; entity_id: string; context: Record<string, unknown>; dedup_key: string }
Export type EnrollResult: { enrollment_id: string; already_enrolled: boolean }
Define internal type StepDef: { id: string; delay: { value: number; unit: 'minutes' | 'hours' | 'days' } } — used to parse version.steps

Export class EnrollmentManager with constructor: (private db: Knex, private definitionsRepo: SequenceDefinitionsRepository, private versionsRepo: SequenceVersionsRepository, private enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository, private stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository, private queue: Queue<StepJobData> | null)
Method enroll(input: EnrollInput): Promise<EnrollResult>
Step 1 — Dedup: call enrollmentsRepo.findByDedupKey(input.dedup_key); if found return { enrollment_id: existing.id, already_enrolled: true } immediately
Step 2 — Load definition: call definitionsRepo.findById(input.sequence_id); if null throw Object.assign(new Error('sequence_not_found'), { code: 'sequence_not_found' })

Step 3 — Validate status: if definition.status === 'disabled' throw Object.assign(new Error('sequence_disabled'), { code: 'sequence_disabled' }); if definition.status !== 'active' || definition.active_version === null throw Object.assign(new Error('sequence_not_active'), { code: 'sequence_not_active' })

Step 4 — Load version: call versionsRepo.findBySequenceAndVersion(input.sequence_id, definition.active_version); if null throw Object.assign(new Error('sequence_version_not_found'), { code: 'sequence_version_not_found' })
Step 5 — Assign variant: call assignVariant(version.ab_test as AbTestConfig | null)

Step 6 — Compute scheduled_at for each step: const UNIT_MS = { minutes: 60_000, hours: 3_600_000, days: 86_400_000 }; const enrolledAt = new Date(); parse version.steps as StepDef[]; for each step at index i: delayMs = step.delay.value * UNIT_MS[step.delay.unit]; scheduledAt = new Date(enrolledAt.getTime() + delayMs)

Step 7 — DB transaction: use this.db.transaction(async (trx) => { const enrollment = await enrollmentsRepo.insert({ sequence_id, sequence_version: definition.active_version, entity_type, entity_id, context, ab_variant, status: 'active', enrolled_at: enrolledAt, dedup_key }, trx); const insertData = steps.map((step, i) => ({ enrollment_id: enrollment.id, step_id: step.id, step_index: i, scheduled_at: scheduledAts[i], job_id: null, status: 'pending' as const, attempt: 0 })); const stepRows = await stepExecutionsRepo.insertMany(insertData, trx); return { enrollment, stepRows }; })

Step 8 — Post-commit enqueue: if this.queue is not null, for each stepRow (in step_index order): call const job = await this.queue.add('execute-step', { enrollment_id: enrollment.id, step_execution_id: stepRow.id, step_id: stepRow.step_id, step_index: stepRow.step_index }, { delay: Math.max(0, stepRow.scheduled_at.getTime() - Date.now()), jobId: crypto.randomUUID() }); then await stepExecutionsRepo.updateJobId(stepRow.id, job.id)
Return { enrollment_id: enrollment.id, already_enrolled: false }
Create apps/platform/nurturing/test/unit/enrollment-manager.test.ts

Use vi.fn() mocks for all repos; mock db.transaction to immediately invoke the callback with a mock trx argument: vi.fn().mockImplementation(async (cb) => cb({})); mock queue.add to return Promise.resolve({ id: 'mock-job-id' })
Test: findByDedupKey returns an existing enrollment → returns { enrollment_id: existing.id, already_enrolled: true }; definitionsRepo.findById not called
Test: definitionsRepo.findById returns null → throws error with code 'sequence_not_found'
Test: definition.status === 'disabled' → throws with code 'sequence_disabled'
Test: definition.status === 'draft' (active_version null) → throws with code 'sequence_not_active'

Test: happy path with 2 steps ({ value: 24, unit: 'hours' } and { value: 72, unit: 'hours' }) → insertMany called with scheduled_at[0] within 100ms of enrolledAt + 86400000 and scheduled_at[1] within 100ms of enrolledAt + 259200000
Test: happy path → queue.add called exactly twice; stepExecutionsRepo.updateJobId called exactly twice
Test: queue is null → queue.add not called; enrollment still inserted; returns { already_enrolled: false }
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 4, originally US-004] Import 'import { randomUUID } from "node:crypto"'. Import 'import type { Knex } from "knex"'. Import Queue from 'bullmq'; import StepJobData from '../queue/step-queue.js'. Import assignVariant and AbTestConfig from './ab-assigner.js'. Import all four repo types from their respective .repo.ts files. The db.transaction() pattern in Knex 3: db.transaction(async (trx) => { ... return result; }) — the promise resolves with the returned value on commit, or rejects and rolls back on throw. For the mock db.transaction in tests: mockDb.transaction = vi.fn().mockImplementation(async (cb: Function) => cb(mockTrx)) where mockTrx is {}. To capture enrolled_at for delta checks in tests, record Date.now() before calling enroll() and compare scheduledAt values against that timestamp.

userStories.title: POST /sequences/enroll route
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the POST /sequences/enroll route so that service-to-service callers (Automation Engine) can enroll entities in sequences over REST.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/enrollments.ts exporting a default Fastify plugin: async function enrollmentsRoutes(fastify: FastifyInstance, opts: EnrollmentsRouteOptions)
Export interface EnrollmentsRouteOptions: { enrollmentManager: EnrollmentManager; enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository; stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository }
POST /sequences/enroll route: preHandler: [fastify.authenticate]

Body schema (TypeBox): { sequence_id: Type.String({ format: 'uuid' }), entity_type: Type.String({ minLength: 1 }), entity_id: Type.String({ minLength: 1 }), context: Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Unknown()), dedup_key: Type.String({ minLength: 1 }) }
Handler: call await opts.enrollmentManager.enroll(request.body); wrap in try/catch
If result.already_enrolled === true → reply.code(200).send({ enrollment_id: result.enrollment_id, already_enrolled: true })

userStories.title: Repository extensions for step worker — enrollment status + step execution lifecycle methods
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need additional repository methods on EnrollmentsRepository and StepExecutionsRepository so that the step worker can perform optimistic-lock claims, lifecycle transitions, and deferral updates atomically.
acceptanceCriteria: In apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/enrollments.repo.ts, add method updateStatus(id: string, status: 'completed' | 'failed', opts?: { completedAt?: Date }): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_enrollments SET status = ?, completed_at = ?, updated_at = now() WHERE id = ?; set completed_at only when opts.completedAt is provided

In apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/step-executions.repo.ts, add method findById(id: string): Promise<SequenceStepExecution | null> — SELECT * FROM sequence_step_executions WHERE id = ? LIMIT 1

Add method claimForExecution(id: string): Promise<boolean> — execute UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET status = 'running', started_at = now() WHERE id = ? AND status = 'pending' RETURNING id; return true if one row returned, false if zero rows (already claimed)

Add method markCompleted(id: string, output: unknown | null): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET status = 'completed', completed_at = now(), output = ? WHERE id = ?; use JSON.stringify for output if not null, store as raw jsonb via Knex
Add method markFailed(id: string, error: string): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET status = 'failed', error = ?, completed_at = now() WHERE id = ?
Add method markCancelled(id: string): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET status = 'cancelled' WHERE id = ? AND status = 'pending'

Add method updateDeferral(id: string, scheduledAt: Date): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET scheduled_at = ?, status = 'pending', job_id = NULL WHERE id = ? (clears job_id so the safety-net poller treats the step as re-enqueued once job_id is written back)
Add method incrementAttempt(id: string): Promise<void> — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET attempt = attempt + 1 WHERE id = ?
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 5, originally US-001] The updateStatus method on enrollments.repo is needed by the step worker to mark enrollment as 'completed' (when the last step finishes) or 'failed' (when max retries exhausted). The claimForExecution method is the optimistic lock — it atomically transitions the step from 'pending' to 'running' and returns false if another worker already claimed it, preventing duplicate execution. updateDeferral sets job_id = NULL so that, after the step worker writes the new job_id back, the safety-net poller can accurately track the step. The existing updateJobId(id, jobId) method from Phase 4 is used for writing the new job_id after re-enqueueing. Import 'import type { Knex } from "knex"' if needed for transaction parameter. In Knex 3 the raw UPDATE...RETURNING * pattern: await this.db('sequence_step_executions').where({ id }).where('status', 'pending').update({ status: 'running', started_at: new Date() }).returning('id') returns an array — length > 0 means the row was claimed.

acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/action-executor.ts
Export interface ActionExecutorDeps: { urls: ServiceUrls; ebClient: EventBridgeClient; busName: string }
Export interface StepDef: { id: string; action: { type: string; params: Record<string, unknown> } }
Export interface ExecutionContext: { enrollment_id: string; step_id: string; entity_type: string; entity_id: string; enrollmentContext: Record<string, unknown>; abVariant: string | null }
Export interface ActionResult: { output?: unknown; chainSendMessage?: { to: string; from: string; body: string; dedup_key: string } }
Export async function executeAction(stepDef: StepDef, execCtx: ExecutionContext, deps: ActionExecutorDeps): Promise<ActionResult>

Import interpolateFields from '@ortho/interpolator'; import ServiceUrls from '../config/service-urls.js'; import executeSendMessage from './action-handlers/send-message.js'; import executeSendEmail from './action-handlers/send-email.js'; import executeCallAi from './action-handlers/call-ai.js'; import executeEmitEvent from './action-handlers/emit-event.js'

Build resolveValue helper inside executeAction: (value: unknown) => resolves string values using interpolateFields(value, { context: execCtx.enrollmentContext, enrollment_id: execCtx.enrollment_id, step_id: execCtx.step_id, entity_type: execCtx.entity_type, entity_id: execCtx.entity_id }); non-string values pass through unchanged

Build resolvedParams: Record<string, unknown> by calling resolveValue on every value in stepDef.action.params using Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(stepDef.action.params).map(([k, v]) => [k, resolveValue(v)]))

If stepDef has ab_variant_override and execCtx.abVariant is non-null: apply overrides — merge Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(stepDef.ab_variant_override[execCtx.abVariant] ?? {}).map(([k, v]) => [k, resolveValue(v)])) into resolvedParams, then re-apply resolveValue to each newly merged key's value
StepDef type should include optional ab_variant_override?: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>> field
Switch on stepDef.action.type:

Case 'send_message': call executeSendMessage({ template_id: resolvedParams.template_id as string, to: resolvedParams.to_field as string, from: resolvedParams.from_field as string, dedup_key: resolvedParams.dedup_key as string, context: execCtx.enrollmentContext }, deps.urls.templateServiceUrl, deps.urls.messagingServiceUrl); return {}

Case 'send_email': call executeSendEmail({ template_id: resolvedParams.template_id as string, to: resolvedParams.to_field as string, from: resolvedParams.from_field as string, dedup_key: resolvedParams.dedup_key as string, context: execCtx.enrollmentContext }, deps.urls.templateServiceUrl, deps.urls.emailServiceUrl); return {}

Case 'call_ai': call executeCallAi({ system_prompt: resolvedParams.system_prompt as string, user_prompt: resolvedParams.user_prompt as string, model: resolvedParams.model as string, auto_send: resolvedParams.auto_send as boolean | undefined }, deps.urls.aiServiceUrl); if result.auto_send === true and resolvedParams.to_field and resolvedParams.from_field and resolvedParams.dedup_key exist, return { output: result.output, chainSendMessage: { to: resolvedParams.to_field as string, from: resolvedParams.from_field as string, body: result.output, dedup_key: resolvedParams.dedup_key as string } }; otherwise return { output: result.output }

Case 'emit_event': call executeEmitEvent({ event_type: resolvedParams.event_type as string, payload: resolvedParams.payload as Record<string, unknown>, include_context: resolvedParams.include_context as boolean | undefined }, { enrollment_id: execCtx.enrollment_id, step_id: execCtx.step_id, entity_type: execCtx.entity_type, entity_id: execCtx.entity_id, enrollmentContext: execCtx.enrollmentContext }, deps.ebClient, deps.busName); return {}
Default: throw new Error(`Unknown action type: ${stepDef.action.type}`)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 5, originally US-007] The execCtx.abVariant is the variant stored on the enrollment row ('A', 'B', or null). The ab_variant_override merge: first merge overrides into resolvedParams (already-resolved base), then resolveValue is applied again to the newly merged keys so any override values that are paths/template strings also get resolved. The chainSendMessage return value allows the step worker to chain a send_message after call_ai without circular imports — the step worker owns the chaining logic. The execCtx.enrollmentContext is passed directly (not spread), preserving it as a named key 'context' for path resolution like 'context.phone'.

userStories.title: Step worker — BullMQ worker process
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the step worker that processes BullMQ jobs from the nurturing:step-execution queue so that step actions are executed at their scheduled time with guard checks, active hours deferral, and retry-safe failure handling.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/step-worker.ts
Import Worker, Queue, Job from 'bullmq'; import IORedis from 'ioredis'; import type { Knex } from 'knex'; import interpolateFields, computeNextActiveWindowMs from '@ortho/interpolator'

Export interface StepWorkerDeps: { db: Knex; enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository; versionsRepo: SequenceVersionsRepository; stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository; queue: Queue<StepJobData>; publisher: NurturingPublisher; actionExecutorDeps: ActionExecutorDeps }
Export function createStepWorker(redisUrl: string, deps: StepWorkerDeps): Worker<StepJobData>
Create the Worker with queue name 'nurturing:step-execution', the processor function, connection: new IORedis(redisUrl, { maxRetriesPerRequest: null, enableReadyCheck: false })
Processor function async (job: Job<StepJobData>): Promise<void> with the following ordered steps:

Step 1 — Load enrollment: const enrollment = await deps.enrollmentsRepo.findById(job.data.enrollment_id); if enrollment is null or enrollment.status !== 'active': await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.markCancelled(job.data.step_execution_id); return (ACK job, stop)
Step 2 — Optimistic lock: const claimed = await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.claimForExecution(job.data.step_execution_id); if claimed === false: return (ACK job, stop — another worker claimed this step)

Step 3 — Load step definition: load the version row via deps.versionsRepo.findBySequenceAndVersion(enrollment.sequence_id, enrollment.sequence_version); parse steps as StepDef[] from version.steps; find the step with id === job.data.step_id; if not found, throw new Error('step_definition_not_found')

Step 4 — Build execution context: const execCtx: ExecutionContext = { enrollment_id: enrollment.id, step_id: stepDef.id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id, enrollmentContext: enrollment.context as Record<string, unknown>, abVariant: enrollment.ab_variant }

Step 5 — Active hours check (send_message and send_email only): if version.active_hours is non-null AND stepDef.action.type is 'send_message' or 'send_email': resolve the timezone by calling interpolateFields(version.active_hours.timezone_field, { context: enrollment.context }); call const delayMs = computeNextActiveWindowMs(version.active_hours, timezone); if delayMs > 0: const newScheduledAt = new Date(Date.now() + delayMs); await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.updateDeferral(job.data.step_execution_id, newScheduledAt); const newJob = await deps.queue.add('execute-step', job.data, { delay: delayMs, jobId: randomUUID() }); await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.updateJobId(job.data.step_execution_id, newJob.id); return (ACK original job)
Step 6 — Execute action: wrap in try/catch; const result = await executeAction(stepDef, execCtx, deps.actionExecutorDeps)

Step 6a — If action was call_ai and result.chainSendMessage is set: call executeSendMessage({ template_id: undefined as unknown as string, to: result.chainSendMessage.to, from: result.chainSendMessage.from, dedup_key: result.chainSendMessage.dedup_key, context: execCtx.enrollmentContext }, deps.actionExecutorDeps.urls.templateServiceUrl, deps.actionExecutorDeps.urls.messagingServiceUrl) — but since body is already known, the send-message handler must be called differently; instead, POST directly to messagingServiceUrl: fetch(`${deps.actionExecutorDeps.urls.messagingServiceUrl}/messages/send`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ body: result.chainSendMessage.body, to: result.chainSendMessage.to, from: result.chainSendMessage.from, dedup_key: result.chainSendMessage.dedup_key }) })
Step 7 — Mark step completed: await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.markCompleted(job.data.step_execution_id, result.output ?? null)

Step 8 — Publish step_output_ready if applicable: if stepDef.action.type === 'call_ai' AND (stepDef.action.params['auto_send'] !== true): await deps.publisher.publishStepOutputReady({ enrollment_id: enrollment.id, step_id: stepDef.id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id })

Step 9 — Check if last step: load all step executions via deps.stepExecutionsRepo.findByEnrollmentId(enrollment.id); if every step has status === 'completed': await deps.enrollmentsRepo.updateStatus(enrollment.id, 'completed', { completedAt: new Date() }); await deps.publisher.publishEnrollmentCompleted({ enrollment_id: enrollment.id, sequence_id: enrollment.sequence_id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id, completed_at: new Date().toISOString() })

On error (catch block): await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.incrementAttempt(job.data.step_execution_id); if job.attemptsMade >= 4 (zero-indexed, 5th attempt = final): await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.markFailed(job.data.step_execution_id, String(err)); await deps.enrollmentsRepo.updateStatus(enrollment.id, 'failed'); await deps.publisher.publishStepFailed({ enrollment_id: enrollment.id, step_id: job.data.step_id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id, error: String(err), attempt: job.attemptsMade + 1 }); throw err (BullMQ moves job to failed list after max retries)
Import { randomUUID } from 'node:crypto'

Import executeAction and ExecutionContext from './action-executor.js'; import executeSendMessage from './action-handlers/send-message.js'; import createStepQueue and StepJobData from '../queue/step-queue.js'; import NurturingPublisher from '../events/publisher.js'; import EnrollmentsRepository from '../repositories/enrollments.repo.js'; import SequenceVersionsRepository from '../repositories/sequence-versions.repo.js'; import StepExecutionsRepository from '../repositories/step-executions.repo.js'; import ActionExecutorDeps from './action-executor.js'
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 5, originally US-008] The retry configuration (5 total attempts, exponential backoff) is set on the BullMQ job when it is enqueued by the enrollment manager. Update apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/enrollment-manager.ts: in the queue.add() call, add options: { delay: ..., jobId: ..., attempts: 5, backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 5000 } }. In BullMQ, job.attemptsMade is zero-indexed: 0 on first attempt, 4 on fifth. The active_hours.timezone_field is a dot-notation path like 'context.location_timezone' — interpolateFields resolves it against { context: enrollment.context }. The computeNextActiveWindowMs from @ortho/interpolator returns 0 when the current time is inside the window (proceed immediately) and a positive ms value when outside (defer). The step in Step 6a bypasses template rendering because the AI already generated the body text — post directly to Messaging Service without Template Service. The enrollment.id is available in the error catch block only if enrollment was loaded successfully in Step 1; if enrollment is null, there is no enrollment to mark failed (the step was already cancelled in Step 1).

userStories.notes: [Phase 6, originally US-002] The trx parameter on findActiveByEntity, markUnenrolled, and cancelPendingByEnrollment enables the unenrollment service to run all three within a single Knex transaction using deps.db.transaction(async (trx) => { ... }). findAllActiveByEntityId has no trx parameter because the opt-out consumer calls it outside any transaction — it iterates the results and calls unenroll() per enrollment, each with its own transaction. The cancelPendingByEnrollment RETURNING clause returns an array of row objects in Knex 3 (not raw scalars), so map with r.job_id. The filter to non-null job_ids is important: steps with job_id = NULL were never enqueued (crash recovery path), so there is nothing to remove from BullMQ for them. userStories.title: Unenrollment service
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a unenrollment service that atomically cancels an enrollment and its pending steps so that callers can unenroll entities from sequences without knowing the enrollment ID.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/unenrollment.ts
Export interface UnenrollParams: { sequence_id: string; entity_type: string; entity_id: string }
Export interface UnenrollmentDeps: { db: Knex; enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository; stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository; stepQueue: Queue; publisher: NurturingPublisher }
Export interface UnenrollResult: { found: boolean; enrollment_id?: string }
Export async function unenroll(params: UnenrollParams, deps: UnenrollmentDeps): Promise<UnenrollResult>

Step 1 — Guard: const enrollment = await deps.enrollmentsRepo.findActiveByEntity(params.sequence_id, params.entity_type, params.entity_id); if (!enrollment) return { found: false } — idempotent no-op, no side effects

Step 2 — Atomic transaction: const jobIds = await deps.db.transaction(async (trx) => { await deps.enrollmentsRepo.markUnenrolled(enrollment.id, trx); return deps.stepExecutionsRepo.cancelPendingByEnrollment(enrollment.id, trx); })
Step 3 — Best-effort BullMQ job removal: for (const jobId of jobIds) { try { const job = await deps.stepQueue.getJob(jobId); await job?.remove(); } catch (err) { /* log warning, do not throw */ } }

Step 4 — Publish event: await deps.publisher.publishEnrollmentUnenrolled({ enrollment_id: enrollment.id, sequence_id: enrollment.sequence_id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id })
Step 5 — Return: return { found: true, enrollment_id: enrollment.id }

Import type { Knex } from 'knex'; import { Queue } from 'bullmq'; import type EnrollmentsRepository from '../repositories/enrollments.repo.js'; import type StepExecutionsRepository from '../repositories/step-executions.repo.js'; import type { NurturingPublisher } from '../events/publisher.js'
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 6, originally US-003] The transaction atomically sets enrollment status = 'unenrolled' AND cancels all pending steps. Any step already in 'running' status is untouched (cancelPendingByEnrollment only touches status = 'pending'). If a running step worker later calls enrollmentsRepo.findById and sees status = 'unenrolled', it was already past the Step 1 guard check in the step worker (which checks before the optimistic lock) — this is fine; the step completes normally. The best-effort BullMQ removal is wrapped in individual try/catch per job_id to ensure a failed removal does not abort the rest. If a removed job is somehow still fired later, the step worker guard (Step 1: enrollment.status !== 'active') will cancel it cleanly. The stepQueue type is Queue<StepJobData> imported from '../queue/step-queue.js' — use the same type as in Phase 5's enrollment manager. The publisher.publishEnrollmentUnenrolled is called AFTER the transaction commits, not inside it, to avoid holding the DB transaction open during an external HTTP call.

Import SQSClient, ReceiveMessageCommand, DeleteMessageCommand from '@aws-sdk/client-sqs'; verify @aws-sdk/client-sqs is in apps/platform/nurturing/package.json dependencies; add it (npm install from that directory) if missing

Export interface OptOutConsumerDeps: { sqsClient: SQSClient; queueUrl: string; enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository; unenroll: (params: UnenrollParams, deps: UnenrollmentDeps) => Promise<UnenrollResult>; unenrollDeps: UnenrollmentDeps; publisher: NurturingPublisher; logger: Logger }
Export async function processOptOutMessage(messageBody: string, deps: OptOutConsumerDeps): Promise<void> — this is the unit-testable core processing function, separate from the polling loop

Inside processOptOutMessage: parse JSON body; validate that parsed.detail exists and typeof parsed.detail.entity_id === 'string'; if validation fails, deps.logger.warn({ body: messageBody }, 'opt_out.received: malformed event, skipping') and return without throwing
const entityId = parsed.detail.entity_id
const enrollments = await deps.enrollmentsRepo.findAllActiveByEntityId(entityId)

For each enrollment: await deps.unenroll({ sequence_id: enrollment.sequence_id, entity_type: enrollment.entity_type, entity_id: enrollment.entity_id }, deps.unenrollDeps) — each call runs its own transaction

After all enrollments processed: await deps.publisher.publishAllSequencesCancelled({ entity_type: enrollments[0]?.entity_type ?? 'unknown', entity_id: entityId, cancelled_count: enrollments.length }) — publish even if cancelled_count === 0 so Lead Service can reliably set opt-out flag

Export function startOptOutConsumer(deps: OptOutConsumerDeps): () => void — starts a polling loop using setInterval at 10-second intervals; each tick: calls SQSClient.send(new ReceiveMessageCommand({ QueueUrl: deps.queueUrl, MaxNumberOfMessages: 10, WaitTimeSeconds: 20 })); for each message: try { await processOptOutMessage(message.Body!, deps); await sqsClient.send(new DeleteMessageCommand({ QueueUrl: deps.queueUrl, ReceiptHandle: message.ReceiptHandle! })); } catch (err) { deps.logger.error(err, 'opt_out consumer: failed to process message, leaving in queue for retry') }; returns a cleanup function that calls clearInterval on the timer
In apps/platform/nurturing/worker.ts, import startOptOutConsumer and call it after the BullMQ step worker is created; pass OPT_OUT_QUEUE_URL from process.env (throw if missing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 6, originally US-005] EventBridge messages delivered to SQS are wrapped in an EventBridge envelope. The message.Body is a JSON string with shape: { version: '0', id: '...', 'detail-type': 'opt_out.received', source: 'platform.messaging', time: '...', detail: { entity_id: '...', phone: '...' } }. The consumer only needs detail.entity_id. Validation must handle missing detail, missing entity_id, and JSON parse errors — all malformed messages must be deleted from SQS after logging (not left to retry infinitely). The long-poll WaitTimeSeconds: 20 on ReceiveMessageCommand is SQS long-polling — it blocks up to 20 seconds per call, reducing empty polls. The startOptOutConsumer uses setInterval as a simple driver; each poll tick is fire-and-forget (not awaited) to prevent the interval from falling behind. The entity_type in publishAllSequencesCancelled is taken from the first enrollment; if no enrollments existed, 'unknown' is acceptable (the event is still useful to Lead Service as a signal to set opt-out). Check if @aws-sdk/client-sqs is already a workspace dep (other services like the Automation Engine may already use it).

userStories.title: Unit tests for unenrollment service
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need unit tests for the unenrollment service covering the happy path and idempotent no-op path so that I can verify cancellation logic in isolation.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/unit/unenrollment.test.ts in apps/platform/nurturing/
Use Vitest (vi.fn()) to mock all dependencies: enrollmentsRepo, stepExecutionsRepo, db.transaction, stepQueue, publisher
For db.transaction mock: vi.fn().mockImplementation(async (fn) => fn(mockTrx)) where mockTrx is a mock Knex.Transaction object — this makes the transaction callback execute synchronously in tests

Test 'unenroll — active enrollment found': mock findActiveByEntity to return a sample enrollment; mock cancelPendingByEnrollment to return ['job-id-1', 'job-id-2']; mock stepQueue.getJob to return { remove: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(undefined) }; call unenroll and assert: markUnenrolled called with enrollment.id and trx; cancelPendingByEnrollment called with enrollment.id and trx; stepQueue.getJob called twice with 'job-id-1' and 'job-id-2'; publishEnrollmentUnenrolled called with correct payload; returns { found: true, enrollment_id: enrollment.id }

Test 'unenroll — leaves non-pending steps untouched': verify cancelPendingByEnrollment is the only step-execution mutation (no markCancelled or markFailed calls) — non-pending steps are excluded by the WHERE status = 'pending' clause in the repo method, which the unit test validates by checking no other step-execution repo methods are called

Test 'unenroll — idempotent when no active enrollment': mock findActiveByEntity to return null; call unenroll and assert: db.transaction NOT called; markUnenrolled NOT called; cancelPendingByEnrollment NOT called; publishEnrollmentUnenrolled NOT called; stepQueue.getJob NOT called; returns { found: false }

Test 'unenroll — BullMQ removal failure does not throw': mock stepQueue.getJob to throw new Error('redis error'); call unenroll; assert it resolves successfully (does not re-throw); publishEnrollmentUnenrolled still called
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 6, originally US-006] The db.transaction mock is the trickiest part. In Knex, db.transaction(async (trx) => { ... }) executes the callback with a transaction object. The mock must call the callback fn(trx) and return the result, so the service's jobIds variable is populated correctly. Use vi.fn().mockImplementation((fn) => fn(mockTrx)) where mockTrx can be a plain object passed as the trx arg to repo methods. The test for 'leaves non-pending steps untouched' is structural — it confirms the service does not call any other step-execution mutation methods beyond cancelPendingByEnrollment. This guarantees non-pending steps are untouched by the service itself (the SQL WHERE clause is the actual guard, which is tested in integration). The BullMQ removal failure test ensures the catch block swallows errors, not re-throws — important because Redis may be unavailable without stopping the unenrollment.

userStories.title: Repository extensions — orphaned step query methods
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need two new query methods on StepExecutionsRepository so that the startup scanner and safety-net poller can find steps that need recovery.
acceptanceCriteria: In apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/step-executions.repo.ts, add method findNullJobIdPending(): Promise<SequenceStepExecution[]> — SELECT * FROM sequence_step_executions WHERE job_id IS NULL AND status = 'pending'; returns all rows matching that condition across all enrollments

Add method findOrphanedPending(): Promise<SequenceStepExecution[]> — SELECT * FROM sequence_step_executions WHERE status = 'pending' AND scheduled_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '1 minute' AND job_id IS NOT NULL; returns steps whose BullMQ job disappeared after initial enqueue

Check whether updateJobId(id: string, jobId: string): Promise<void> already exists on the repository (it was introduced in Phase 4 for post-enqueue write-back); if it does not exist, add it — UPDATE sequence_step_executions SET job_id = ?, updated_at = NOW() WHERE id = ?; if it does exist, do not duplicate it
Both new methods use this.db as the query builder (no transaction parameter — these are read-only background queries)
Return types use the existing SequenceStepExecution interface already defined in the repo file
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-001] findNullJobIdPending is used by the startup scanner: it finds steps where the process crashed between the DB transaction commit (step 5 of enrollment) and the BullMQ enqueue (step 6). These steps have a committed DB row but no BullMQ job. scheduled_at may be in the future (normal) or in the past (crash happened after the step was due). findOrphanedPending is used by the safety-net poller: it finds steps where job_id IS NOT NULL (job was enqueued at some point) but the BullMQ job is now gone (Redis data loss or restart). The 1-minute grace period prevents re-enqueuing steps that are still waiting in BullMQ but whose scheduled_at just passed while the worker was momentarily busy. Both methods scan the global table without filtering by enrollment_id — they recover across all active enrollments. Check Phase 4's enrollment-manager.ts for the updateJobId pattern used post-enqueue to avoid introducing a duplicate.

userStories.title: Startup scanner service
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a startup scanner that re-enqueues all pending steps with null job_id on service boot so that steps that were persisted to DB but never enqueued (due to a crash) are recovered automatically.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/startup-scanner.ts
Export interface StartupScannerDeps: { stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository; stepQueue: Queue<StepJobData>; logger: Logger }
Export async function runStartupScan(deps: StartupScannerDeps): Promise<void>

Implementation: const steps = await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.findNullJobIdPending(); deps.logger.info({ count: steps.length }, 'startup-scanner: found null-job-id pending steps'); if steps.length === 0, return immediately

For each step: compute delayMs = Math.max(0, new Date(step.scheduled_at).getTime() - Date.now()); const job = await deps.stepQueue.add('execute-step', { enrollment_id: step.enrollment_id, step_id: step.step_id }, { delay: delayMs, jobId: step.id }); await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.updateJobId(step.id, job.id!); deps.logger.info({ step_id: step.id, job_id: job.id, delay_ms: delayMs }, 'startup-scanner: re-enqueued step')
Wrap the entire for-loop body in try/catch per step — a failure on one step must not abort recovery of the remaining steps; log the error and continue
After the loop: deps.logger.info({ count: steps.length }, 'startup-scanner: recovery complete')

Import Queue from 'bullmq'; import type StepExecutionsRepository from '../repositories/step-executions.repo.js'; import type { StepJobData } from '../queue/step-queue.js'; import type { Logger } from '@ortho/logger'
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-002] The delayMs computation uses Math.max(0, ...) so that steps whose scheduled_at is already in the past are enqueued with delay 0 (fire immediately). Steps with a future scheduled_at get the correct remaining delay. The jobId option in stepQueue.add is set to step.id (the UUID of the step execution row) — this makes BullMQ job IDs deterministic and idempotent: if runStartupScan is called twice before the job fires, BullMQ will return the existing job rather than creating a duplicate (BullMQ deduplicates on jobId). Check step-queue.ts created in Phase 4/5 for the exact StepJobData interface shape ({ enrollment_id: string; step_id: string }) and Queue instantiation pattern. The per-step try/catch is critical: a Redis error on one step should not prevent recovery of others. The logger is the Pino instance from @ortho/logger — check how it is passed in the enrollment manager to replicate the pattern.

userStories.title: Safety-net poller — Fastify plugin with Redis distributed lock
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a Fastify plugin that polls every 5 minutes for orphaned BullMQ jobs and re-enqueues them so that steps whose Redis job was lost after enqueue are recovered within 6 minutes.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/safety-net-poller.ts
Import fp from 'fastify-plugin'; import Redis from 'ioredis'; import Queue from 'bullmq'; import type StepExecutionsRepository from '../repositories/step-executions.repo.js'
Export interface SafetyNetPollerDeps: { stepExecutionsRepo: StepExecutionsRepository; stepQueue: Queue<StepJobData>; redis: Redis; logger: Logger }
Export async function runPollCycle(deps: SafetyNetPollerDeps): Promise<void> — this is the unit-testable core, separate from the Fastify plugin wrapper

runPollCycle implementation: const LOCK_KEY = 'nurturing:safety-net-poller:lock'; const LOCK_TTL_MS = 240000; const acquired = await deps.redis.set(LOCK_KEY, '1', 'PX', LOCK_TTL_MS, 'NX'); if (acquired !== 'OK') { deps.logger.debug('safety-net-poller: lock not acquired, skipping cycle'); return; }
After acquiring lock: const steps = await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.findOrphanedPending(); deps.logger.info({ count: steps.length }, 'safety-net-poller: found orphaned steps')

For each step: compute delayMs = 0 (all orphaned steps have scheduled_at in the past per the query condition); const job = await deps.stepQueue.add('execute-step', { enrollment_id: step.enrollment_id, step_id: step.step_id }, { delay: 0, jobId: step.id }); await deps.stepExecutionsRepo.updateJobId(step.id, job.id!); deps.logger.info({ step_id: step.id }, 'safety-net-poller: re-enqueued orphaned step')
Wrap per-step logic in try/catch — log and continue on individual step failure

Export default fp(async function safetyNetPollerPlugin(fastify, opts: SafetyNetPollerDeps) { const timer = setInterval(() => { void runPollCycle(opts).catch(err => fastify.log.error(err, 'safety-net-poller: poll cycle error')); }, 5 * 60 * 1000); fastify.addHook('onClose', async () => { clearInterval(timer); }); }, { name: 'safety-net-poller' })
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-003] The Redis lock (SET NX PX) ensures only one ECS instance runs the poll cycle at a time. LOCK_TTL_MS is set to 240000 (4 minutes) — shorter than the 5-minute poll interval so the lock expires before the next cycle even if the process holding it crashes mid-cycle. The ioredis client is the same Redis connection used by BullMQ — in Phase 4/5, BullMQ was instantiated with a connection option; check whether a bare ioredis client is already exported from src/queue/step-queue.ts or src/index.ts. If BullMQ was configured with a connection object literal rather than an ioredis instance, a separate ioredis instance needs to be created with the same REDIS_URL. The jobId: step.id pattern in stepQueue.add ensures BullMQ deduplication if the poller runs while the original job is actually still in Redis (shouldn't happen given the query condition, but is safe). fastify-plugin (fp) wraps the plugin to avoid Fastify's encapsulation boundary — it allows the plugin to access decorators from the parent scope. Check if fastify-plugin is already in package.json (it was likely added for other plugins in Phase 2/5).

userStories.title: Bootstrap integration — register startup scanner and safety-net poller
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the startup scanner to run on service boot and the safety-net poller to be registered as a Fastify plugin so that recovery mechanisms are active in production.
acceptanceCriteria: In apps/platform/nurturing/src/index.ts, import runStartupScan from './services/startup-scanner.js' and the safetyNetPollerPlugin default export from './services/safety-net-poller.js'

Register the safety-net poller plugin before calling fastify.listen: await fastify.register(safetyNetPollerPlugin, { stepExecutionsRepo, stepQueue, redis, logger: fastify.log }); — pass the same repo, queue, and redis instances used elsewhere in the server

After await fastify.listen(...) returns (server is now accepting traffic), fire the startup scanner as a non-blocking background task: void runStartupScan({ stepExecutionsRepo, stepQueue, logger: fastify.log }).catch(err => fastify.log.error(err, 'startup-scanner: unhandled error'))
The startup scanner call must NOT be awaited before fastify.listen — it runs after the server is ready and does not block HTTP traffic

The redis instance passed to safetyNetPollerPlugin must be the same ioredis client connection used by BullMQ (or a new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL) if no bare client is already exported); if creating a new client, throw on missing REDIS_URL
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-004] The ordering is critical: fastify.register(safetyNetPollerPlugin) before fastify.listen so the plugin's onClose hook is registered in the Fastify lifecycle. The startup scanner runs after fastify.listen resolves so the server is already accepting health checks before the scan begins — this is the non-blocking requirement from spec Section 5.5. Check src/index.ts for the existing pattern of how stepExecutionsRepo and stepQueue are instantiated; the same variables should be passed directly to the plugin and scanner without creating new instances. If the server uses a graceful shutdown hook (SIGTERM), verify clearInterval is called via the onClose hook registered in safetyNetPollerPlugin — Fastify calls onClose hooks during fastify.close().

userStories.title: Integration tests — startup scanner
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need integration tests for the startup scanner so that I can verify it correctly re-enqueues null-job-id steps and that the server starts accepting traffic before the scan completes.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/integration/startup-scanner.test.ts in apps/platform/nurturing/

Test setup: create an active sequence with 1 step; insert an enrollment with 1 step execution row directly into DB with status='pending' and job_id=NULL and scheduled_at=NOW() + interval '1 hour' (simulating a crash between DB commit and BullMQ enqueue)

Test 'startup-scanner: re-enqueues null-job-id pending step': call runStartupScan({ stepExecutionsRepo, stepQueue: mockStepQueue, logger }) directly; assert that mockStepQueue.add was called once with ('execute-step', { enrollment_id, step_id: ... }, expect.objectContaining({ jobId: step.id })); query DB and assert step.job_id is now set to the mocked job.id returned by stepQueue.add

Test 'startup-scanner: skips steps with existing job_id': insert a second step execution row with job_id='existing-job' and status='pending'; call runStartupScan; assert stepQueue.add was called exactly once (only the null-job-id step), not twice
Test 'startup-scanner: handles empty result — no-op when no null-job-id steps exist': ensure all step_execution rows have non-null job_ids; call runStartupScan; assert stepQueue.add was never called

mockStepQueue is a Vitest mock: const mockStepQueue = { add: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue({ id: 'mock-job-id' }), getJob: vi.fn() } — typed as Queue<StepJobData>
