userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-005] The 'server accepts traffic before scan completes' test is the most architecturally important one — it verifies the non-blocking design. The simplest way to implement it: create a slow mock scanner (returns a Promise that resolves after a timeout), start the server with that mock, make an HTTP request to /health (or the sequences list endpoint) immediately, assert the response arrives before the scanner resolves. Use a short artificial delay (e.g. 200ms) in the mock so the test completes quickly. The DB test for re-enqueueing uses runStartupScan directly (not through the HTTP server) — faster and avoids needing a full Fastify instance. The mockStepQueue.add mock returns { id: 'mock-job-id' } — match the BullMQ Job type's id property (it is a string). Check Phase 4 integration tests for the DB setup pattern (how enrollments and step_executions are inserted directly).

userStories.title: Integration tests — safety-net poller
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need integration tests for the safety-net poller so that I can verify orphaned steps are re-enqueued, the Redis lock prevents concurrent runs, and the dedup_key prevents duplicate outbound sends.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/integration/safety-net-poller.test.ts in apps/platform/nurturing/

Test setup: create an active sequence; insert an enrollment with 1 step execution row with status='pending', scheduled_at = NOW() - interval '2 minutes', job_id = 'lost-job-123' (simulating a BullMQ job that was enqueued but disappeared from Redis)

Test 'safety-net-poller: re-enqueues orphaned step': call runPollCycle({ stepExecutionsRepo, stepQueue: mockStepQueue, redis: mockRedis, logger }) directly; assert mockStepQueue.add was called once with ('execute-step', { enrollment_id, step_id: ... }, expect.objectContaining({ delay: 0, jobId: step.id })); query DB and assert step.job_id is updated to the mocked job.id

Test 'safety-net-poller: skips steps not yet overdue': insert a step with scheduled_at = NOW() + interval '30 seconds' and job_id IS NOT NULL; call runPollCycle; assert stepQueue.add was NOT called (step is still within the 1-minute grace window)

Test 'safety-net-poller: skips null-job-id steps': insert a step with job_id = NULL and scheduled_at in the past; call runPollCycle; assert stepQueue.add was NOT called (null-job-id steps are handled by the startup scanner, not the poller)

Test 'safety-net-poller: Redis lock prevents concurrent cycle': call runPollCycle twice concurrently; mock mockRedis.set to return 'OK' on first call and null on second (lock already held); assert stepQueue.add called exactly once (second cycle skipped due to lock)

Test 'safety-net-poller: dedup_key ensures no duplicate send — re-enqueued step uses same jobId as original step.id': assert stepQueue.add is called with jobId equal to step.id (UUID); this ensures BullMQ deduplicates if the original job somehow reappears; note this is a structural assertion, not an end-to-end send test
mockRedis is a Vitest mock: const mockRedis = { set: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue('OK') } — typed as Redis
mockStepQueue is a Vitest mock: const mockStepQueue = { add: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue({ id: 'mock-job-id' }) } — typed as Queue<StepJobData>
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 7, originally US-006] runPollCycle is tested directly (not through the Fastify plugin wrapper) for the same reason processOptOutMessage was tested directly in Phase 6 — it avoids needing a live setInterval and makes test execution fast and deterministic. The dedup_key no-duplicate-send test is a structural assertion about jobId usage rather than a full end-to-end test: the spec says 'no duplicate send (Messaging Service dedup_key guard)' — in practice this means both the step worker's dedup_key field AND BullMQ's job deduplication on jobId = step.id prevent double execution. The test verifies the latter (correct jobId) because the former (dedup_key passed to Messaging Service) is already covered by Phase 5 step-worker integration tests. The 'Redis lock prevents concurrent cycle' test uses Promise.all([runPollCycle(deps), runPollCycle(deps)]) with mock.set returning 'OK' then null in sequence — verify with mockRedis.set.mock.calls.length === 2 and stepQueue.add.mock.calls.length === 1.

userStories.title: Migration — sequence_conversions table for A/B tracking
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a DB table to record A/B test conversions so that the stats endpoint can compute per-variant conversion rates.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/migrations/005_sequence_conversions.ts

Migration creates table sequence_conversions in the platform_nurturing schema: id uuid PRIMARY KEY, enrollment_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES sequence_enrollments(id), sequence_id uuid NOT NULL, ab_variant text, entity_type text NOT NULL, entity_id text NOT NULL, event_type text NOT NULL, converted_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
Add UNIQUE constraint on (enrollment_id) — one conversion recorded per enrollment (first matching event wins)
Add index: CREATE INDEX ON sequence_conversions (sequence_id, ab_variant) for stats aggregation queries
Add index: CREATE INDEX ON sequence_conversions (entity_id, event_type) for the consumer lookup
Migration is reversible — down() drops the table
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-001] The sequence_conversions table is separate from sequence_enrollments to keep the enrollment row narrow and allow multiple future conversion types if needed. The UNIQUE constraint on enrollment_id means only the first conversion event per enrollment is recorded — consistent with treating A/B conversion as a binary outcome. The ab_variant column is denormalized from enrollment_id for faster aggregation in the stats query (avoids joining back to sequence_enrollments). sequence_id is also denormalized for the same reason. userStories.title: Conversion event consumer — record A/B conversions from EventBridge
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need an SQS consumer that listens for tracked_event events and records conversions for enrolled A/B test entities so that the stats API has accurate conversion_count data.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/conversions.repo.ts exporting class ConversionsRepository with constructor(private db: Knex)
ConversionsRepository.findByEnrollmentId(enrollmentId: string): Promise<{ id: string } | undefined> — SELECT id FROM sequence_conversions WHERE enrollment_id = $1 LIMIT 1

ConversionsRepository.insert(row: { id: string; enrollment_id: string; sequence_id: string; ab_variant: string | null; entity_type: string; entity_id: string; event_type: string; converted_at: Date }): Promise<void> — INSERT INTO sequence_conversions ... ON CONFLICT (enrollment_id) DO NOTHING

Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/consumers/conversion.consumer.ts exporting async function processConversionEvent(event: unknown, deps: { enrollmentsRepo: EnrollmentsRepository; conversionsRepo: ConversionsRepository; logger: Logger }): Promise<void>
processConversionEvent: validate event has event_type string and entity_id string — if malformed, log warning and return without throwing

Look up all active enrollments for entity_id across all sequences: SELECT se.* FROM sequence_enrollments se JOIN sequence_versions sv ON (sv.sequence_id = se.sequence_id AND sv.version = se.sequence_version) WHERE se.entity_id = $1 AND se.status = 'active' AND sv.ab_test IS NOT NULL AND sv.ab_test->>'enabled' = 'true'

For each matching enrollment: check if event_type matches sv.ab_test.tracked_event AND evaluate tracked_condition using the @platform/filter-engine evaluator (or a simple field/op/value check inline) against the event payload — if both match AND no conversion already recorded for this enrollment_id, insert a conversion row
If already converted (findByEnrollmentId returns a row), skip silently — idempotent
Validate + handle malformed events without crashing (try/catch wrapping the entire body)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-002] The conversion consumer's SQS queue registration follows the same pattern as the opt-out consumer introduced in Phase 6 (src/consumers/opt-out.consumer.ts). The consumer is wired into the worker entry point (worker.ts) or into index.ts if the project bootstraps all consumers there — check how processOptOutMessage is invoked in Phase 6 to replicate the pattern. The tracked_condition evaluation only needs to support the subset of operators defined in @ortho/types / @platform/filter-engine: eq, neq, gt, lt, contains. The @platform/filter-engine package already implements evaluateCondition — import and reuse it rather than re-implementing. If the package is not available or its interface differs, implement a minimal inline evaluator for the three operators needed. The ab_test.tracked_event and ab_test.tracked_condition fields live in the sequence_versions row (sv.ab_test JSONB column). Parse with JSON.parse if Knex does not auto-parse JSONB.

userStories.title: Stats repository — enrollment counts and A/B breakdown queries
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need repository query methods that aggregate enrollment and conversion counts for a sequence so that the stats route can compute all required metrics in one pass.
acceptanceCriteria: Add method getEnrollmentCounts(sequenceId: string): Promise<{ total: number; completed: number; unenrolled: number; failed: number; active: number }> to EnrollmentsRepository (apps/platform/nurturing/src/repositories/enrollments.repo.ts)

getEnrollmentCounts implementation: SELECT COUNT(*) as total, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'completed') as completed, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'unenrolled') as unenrolled, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'failed') as failed, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') as active FROM sequence_enrollments WHERE sequence_id = $1
Add method getAbBreakdown(sequenceId: string): Promise<Array<{ ab_variant: string; enrollments: number; completions: number; conversions: number }>> to EnrollmentsRepository

getAbBreakdown implementation: SELECT se.ab_variant, COUNT(*) as enrollments, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE se.status = 'completed') as completions, COUNT(sc.id) as conversions FROM sequence_enrollments se LEFT JOIN sequence_conversions sc ON sc.enrollment_id = se.id WHERE se.sequence_id = $1 AND se.ab_variant IS NOT NULL GROUP BY se.ab_variant
Both methods return parsed numbers (not strings) — use parseInt() or cast in SQL since PostgreSQL COUNT returns bigint as string in node-postgres
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-003] PostgreSQL COUNT(*) returns a bigint which node-postgres serialises as a string. Wrap each count column with parseInt(row.total, 10) etc. after the query, or use CAST(COUNT(*) AS int) in the SQL. Check how the enrollments repo handles numeric columns elsewhere in the project to replicate the pattern. The getAbBreakdown query groups by ab_variant — only rows where ab_variant IS NOT NULL are included (enrollments from sequences with no A/B test have ab_variant = null and are excluded from the A/B block). ConversionsRepository is imported in enrollments.repo.ts only if needed for a joined query — otherwise the JOIN is done inline in getAbBreakdown since both tables are in the same schema.

userStories.title: Z-test utility + unit tests
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need a pure function that computes a two-proportion z-test so that the stats endpoint can determine A/B winner significance with correct p-values.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/services/ab-significance.ts
Export interface AbVariantStats { enrollments: number; conversions: number }
Export interface AbSignificanceResult { significant: boolean; winner: string | null; p_value: number }
Export function computeAbSignificance(a: AbVariantStats, b: AbVariantStats, variantAName: string, variantBName: string): AbSignificanceResult
Implementation: if a.enrollments < 100 OR b.enrollments < 100, return { significant: false, winner: null, p_value: 1 }
Compute p1 = a.conversions / a.enrollments, p2 = b.conversions / b.enrollments
Compute pooled proportion: p_pool = (a.conversions + b.conversions) / (a.enrollments + b.enrollments)
Compute standard error: se = Math.sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1 / a.enrollments + 1 / b.enrollments))
If se === 0, return { significant: false, winner: null, p_value: 1 }
Compute z = Math.abs(p1 - p2) / se

Compute two-tailed p-value from z using the standard normal CDF approximation: p_value = 2 * (1 - normalCdf(z)); implement normalCdf(z) using the Abramowitz and Stegun approximation (error function): 0.5 * erfc(-z / Math.SQRT2) where erfc is approximated as 1 - erf(x) using the polynomial from A&S 7.1.26 with coefficients [0.0705230784, 0.0422820123, 0.0092705272, 0.0001520143, 0.0002765672, 0.0000430638]
significant = p_value < 0.05; winner = significant ? (p1 >= p2 ? variantAName : variantBName) : null
Create test/unit/ab-significance.test.ts
Test: both variants below 100 enrollments → significant: false, winner: null, p_value: 1
Test: variant A conversion rate 0.24 (42/175), variant B 0.177 (31/175) → p_value approximately 0.031, significant: true, winner: 'A' (matches design spec example)
Test: identical conversion rates (50/175 each) → significant: false, winner: null
Test: se = 0 edge case (0 conversions in both variants) → significant: false, winner: null, p_value: 1
Test: exactly 100 enrollments per variant → minimum threshold satisfied (does not return early)
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-004] The Abramowitz and Stegun erf approximation (A&S 7.1.26) is accurate to ±1.5e-7 which is more than sufficient for a p-value threshold of 0.05. The p-value for the design spec example (42/175 vs 31/175) is stated as 0.031 — use this as the primary regression test. The approximation implementation: function erf(x: number): number { const t = 1 / (1 + 0.3275911 * Math.abs(x)); const poly = t * (0.254829592 + t * (-0.284496736 + t * (1.421413741 + t * (-1.453152027 + t * 1.061405429)))); const result = 1 - poly * Math.exp(-x * x); return x >= 0 ? result : -result; } — this is the simpler 5-term version (A&S 7.1.26). Implement normalCdf as: function normalCdf(z: number): number { return 0.5 * (1 + erf(z / Math.SQRT2)); }. No external statistics library — pure TS implementation only.

userStories.title: Stats route — GET /sequences/:id/stats
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need the GET /sequences/:id/stats endpoint implemented so that consumers can retrieve completion rates and A/B test results for a sequence.
acceptanceCriteria: Create apps/platform/nurturing/src/routes/stats.ts with a single route handler: GET /sequences/:id/stats
Handler: fetch enrollment counts via enrollmentsRepo.getEnrollmentCounts(id) — return 404 if sequence not found (check sequence_definitions first)
Compute rates: completion_rate = completed / total (0 if total = 0), unenrollment_rate = unenrolled / total (0 if total = 0)

Fetch sequence definition to check whether ab_test is configured on the active version: load active_version from sequence_definitions, then sequence_versions row; if ab_test is null or ab_test.enabled = false, set ab: null in response

If A/B test is enabled: fetch ab breakdown via enrollmentsRepo.getAbBreakdown(id); for each variant compute completion_rate = completions / enrollments (0 if 0) and conversion_rate = conversions / enrollments (0 if 0)
Compute statistical significance via computeAbSignificance from ab-significance.ts using the two variants' enrollments + conversions; include p_value, significant, winner in response

Response shape matches spec Section 6.1 exactly: { sequence_id, total_enrollments, completed_count, unenrolled_count, failed_count, active_count, completion_rate, unenrollment_rate, ab: null | { A: {...}, B: {...}, winner, significant, p_value } }
Register route in apps/platform/nurturing/src/index.ts with the same auth middleware (JWT required) as the other sequence routes
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-005] The route needs ConversionsRepository and EnrollmentsRepository injected — check how other routes in src/routes/ receive repository dependencies (likely via Fastify decorators or closure-based factory). The ab_test configuration is on sequence_versions, not sequence_definitions — load the active_version row to check ab_test.enabled. When total = 0, return rates as 0 rather than NaN (division by zero guard). The winner field in the A/B block uses the variant key names from the breakdown ('A' and 'B') — pass those as variantAName and variantBName to computeAbSignificance. Round rates to 3 decimal places: Math.round(rate * 1000) / 1000 — consistent with the spec example values (0.629, 0.571, etc.).

userStories.title: Contract tests — outbound send_message and send_email action handlers
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need contract tests that verify send_message and send_email action handlers call downstream platform services with the exact expected request shapes so that integration breakage is caught at the Nurturing Engine boundary.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/contract/outbound-send-message.contract.test.ts in apps/platform/nurturing/
Use Vitest with vi.fn() / vi.spyOn() mocks on the HTTP client (or MSW handlers if the project uses MSW) to intercept outgoing HTTP calls without hitting real services

Test 'send_message handler: calls POST /templates/render with template_id and context before POST /messages/send': set up a mock enrollment with a send_message step; execute the step via the action handler directly (import SendMessageHandler and call execute()); assert that the first HTTP call is POST /templates/render with body { template_id: '<value from step params>', context: <enrollment context> }; assert that the second HTTP call is POST /messages/send with body { to: '<resolved phone>', from: '<resolved from_number>', body: '<rendered body from template render response>', dedup_key: '<enrollment_id>-step-id' }

Test 'send_message handler: passes pre-rendered body (not template_id) to POST /messages/send': assert that the POST /messages/send body does NOT contain a template_id field — only pre-rendered body string
Test 'send_message handler: dedup_key is present in POST /messages/send body': assert dedup_key is a non-empty string in the send call
Create test/contract/outbound-send-email.contract.test.ts

Test 'send_email handler: calls POST /templates/render then POST /emails/send': assert POST /templates/render called with { template_id, context }; assert POST /emails/send called with { to, from, subject: '<rendered subject>', body_html: '<rendered html>', body_text: '<rendered text>' } — all three body fields present
Test 'send_email handler: passes pre-rendered subject + body_html + body_text (not template_id) to POST /emails/send': assert POST /emails/send body does NOT contain template_id
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes:
[Phase 8, originally US-006] Contract tests verify the request shape sent to downstream services — they do not need a live service. Use vi.fn() or Vitest's spy utilities to intercept the HTTP client used by the action handlers. Check what HTTP client the action handlers use (likely node-fetch, undici, or a thin wrapper imported from a shared package) — mock at that level. The test setup needs a minimal enrollment context object with the fields that send_message and send_email step params reference (phone, location_number, location_timezone, etc. matching the no-response follow-up sequence DSL in spec Section 3.1). The template render mock should return { subject: 'Test Subject', body_html: '

Test

', body_text: 'Test', body: 'Test SMS body' } so send_message and send_email handlers can extract their respective fields. These tests import the action handlers directly (src/services/action-handlers/send-message.ts and send-email.ts) rather than going through the full step worker — faster and more focused.

userStories.title: Contract tests — call_ai handler and EventBridge nurturing.* event shapes
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need contract tests that verify the call_ai handler sends only the required fields to POST /ai/complete and that all published nurturing.* EventBridge events match the @ortho/event-bus schema so that integration contracts are enforced.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/contract/outbound-call-ai.contract.test.ts

Test 'call_ai handler: calls POST /ai/complete with { system_prompt, user_prompt, model } only': mock the HTTP client; execute a call_ai step with params { system_prompt, user_prompt, model, auto_send: false }; assert POST /ai/complete was called with exactly { system_prompt: '<resolved value>', user_prompt: '<resolved value>', model: '<value>' } — no extra fields

Test 'call_ai handler: user_prompt and system_prompt are resolved via @ortho/interpolator before sending': use a prompt containing {{context.first_name}} and an enrollment context with first_name set; assert the resolved string (not the template) was sent to POST /ai/complete
Test 'call_ai handler with auto_send: false: stores AI output and does NOT call POST /messages/send': mock AI response; assert step output is stored; assert no call to POST /messages/send
Test 'call_ai handler with auto_send: true: calls POST /messages/send with AI output as body': mock AI response; assert POST /messages/send called with body = AI response text
Create test/contract/outbound-events.contract.test.ts

For each nurturing.* event published by src/events/publisher.ts — test each event's payload shape against the TypeScript interfaces defined in @ortho/types: nurturing.enrollment_completed, nurturing.enrollment_unenrolled, nurturing.step_failed, nurturing.step_output_ready, nurturing.all_sequences_cancelled

Each event shape test: call the publisher function with a valid payload; capture the EventBridge PutEvents call (mock the @ortho/event-bus client); assert the published event body matches the expected TypeScript interface shape (all required fields present, correct types)
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-007] The 'only { system_prompt, user_prompt, model }' assertion is the most important for the call_ai contract — the spec is explicit that no extra fields are sent. Use expect(capturedBody).toEqual({ system_prompt: ..., user_prompt: ..., model: ... }) rather than expect.objectContaining(...) so extra fields would cause the test to fail. For EventBridge event shape tests, mock the @ortho/event-bus putEvent function (or the underlying AWS EventBridge client) at the module level with vi.mock('@ortho/event-bus'). Capture the call arguments and assert the event body matches the @ortho/types interface fields. The nurturing.* event types should already be defined in @ortho/types from earlier phases — check packages/@ortho/types/src/events/ for the relevant interfaces. If the @ortho/event-bus mock returns a resolved promise, the publisher functions should complete without error.

userStories.title: Contract tests — inbound opt_out.received malformed event handling
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I need contract tests that verify the opt_out.received SQS consumer handles malformed events without crashing so that a single bad message cannot kill the consumer process.
acceptanceCriteria: Create test/contract/inbound-opt-out.contract.test.ts in apps/platform/nurturing/
Import processOptOutMessage from src/consumers/opt-out.consumer.ts (the function implemented in Phase 6)

Test 'handles missing entity_id — does not throw': call processOptOutMessage({ event_type: 'opt_out.received', payload: {} }, deps) and assert it resolves without throwing; assert no unenrollment was attempted
Test 'handles completely invalid JSON structure — does not throw': call processOptOutMessage(null, deps) and assert it resolves without throwing
Test 'handles missing payload — does not throw': call processOptOutMessage({ event_type: 'opt_out.received' }, deps) and assert it resolves without throwing
Test 'handles unexpected event_type — does not throw': call processOptOutMessage({ event_type: 'some.other.event', payload: { entity_id: 'test' } }, deps) and assert it resolves without throwing

Test 'handles valid opt_out.received — calls unenrollment for all active enrollments': set up a mock enrollmentsRepo that returns 2 active enrollments for entity_id 'lead-123'; assert unenroll was called twice with the correct sequence_id and entity_id values; assert nurturing.all_sequences_cancelled was published

Test 'handles unenrollment error on one enrollment — continues processing others and does not throw': mock the first unenrollment to throw; assert the second enrollment unenrollment was still called; assert the consumer resolved without throwing

deps is a minimal mock: { enrollmentsRepo: { findActiveByEntityId: vi.fn() }, unenrollmentService: { unenroll: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(undefined) }, publisher: { publish: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(undefined) }, logger: { info: vi.fn(), warn: vi.fn(), error: vi.fn() } }
Tests pass (npm run test from apps/platform/nurturing)
Typecheck passes (npm run typecheck from apps/platform/nurturing)
userStories.notes: [Phase 8, originally US-008] These are inbound contract tests — they verify that the consumer is robust against malformed SQS messages. SQS standard queues guarantee at-least-once delivery and can deliver duplicate or corrupted messages; a throwing consumer will cause the message to remain in the queue and be redelivered endlessly. The 'handles completely invalid JSON structure — does not throw' test passes null because AWS Lambda SQS event parsing can produce null body in edge cases. Check processOptOutMessage's actual function signature from Phase 6 — the deps parameter shape may differ slightly from the mock above; adjust the mock to match the real interface. The 'continues processing others' test verifies per-enrollment try/catch which was a spec requirement in Phase 6 — this contract test is verifying that requirement is actually met.
